summary:
Paul of Tarsus (c. 5–67 AD) was the most influential missionary and theologian of early Christianity — a Pharisaic Jew from the diaspora who experienced a dramatic conversion on the road to Damascus and became the apostle who spread Christianity across the Gentile Roman world. His three missionary journeys (c. 46–58 AD) established Christian communities throughout Anatolia, Macedonia, Greece, and eventually Rome, and his 13 letters (of which 7 are universally accepted as authentic) form the earliest and theologically richest stratum of the New Testament.

Paul's theological innovations transformed Christianity: his argument that Gentiles need not observe Jewish law to be saved (justified by faith rather than works of the Law) freed the movement from its Jewish ethnic boundaries and enabled universal adoption. His letters articulate a sophisticated theology of sin, grace, faith, the body of Christ (the church), and eschatological expectation that has shaped Christian thought from Augustine to Luther to the present. Paul's insistence on the centrality of the Resurrection as historical event ('if Christ has not been raised, your faith is futile' — 1 Corinthians 15:17) set the terms of Christian apologetics. He was executed in Rome under Nero around 67 AD, according to tradition by beheading (as a Roman citizen, he was spared crucifixion).
